turns.student: How have you been feeling overall lately?
turns.patient: Honestly, I've been feeling pretty crummy. The crampy lower abdominal pain on my left side has been getting worse over the past few weeks, and to be honest, it's really frustrating because nothing seems to be helping - not even loperamide, which is why I'm here now.

turns.student: Tell me about your day-to-day — how has your health been in general?
turns.patient: It's been tough. The diarrhea has been constant, 8-10 bowel movements a day, and it's always loose to watery with this urgency that I just can't get away from.

turns.student: You mentioned some health issues. Can you walk me through what happens?
turns.patient: Well, when I wake up in the morning, I usually have a bowel movement right away, and it sets the tone for the rest of the day. The cramps start to kick in after that, and they can be pretty intense - sometimes they're so bad I feel like I'm going to double over. And then the diarrhea just keeps coming throughout the day, with no real break or relief. It's exhausting trying to manage it all.

turns.student: And when these episodes occur, what does your body do?
turns.patient: When I have a bowel movement, I feel like I'm in a rush because of the urgency - it's like my body is giving me this intense signal that I need to go right now. And then after I've done my business, there's always this burning sensation and sometimes even some blood with the stool, which is really scary for me, to be honest.

turns.student: Let's focus on the episodes. Walk me through one from start to finish.
turns.patient: One of these episodes usually starts early in the morning when I wake up. I'll get this urgent feeling that I need to have a bowel movement, and it's like my body is giving me this intense signal that I need to go right now - it can be pretty overwhelming.

turns.student: During an episode, do you notice anything besides the main symptom?
turns.patient: Yeah, besides the cramps, diarrhea, and urgency, there's also this sensation of incomplete evacuation after most bowel movements. It feels like I'm not quite finishing when I go to the bathroom, even though I've had a bowel movement already. That feeling is really uncomfortable and makes me feel anxious about what's going on with my body.

turns.student: Specifically, any changes in your skin — like sweating or flushing?
turns.patient: To be honest, I haven't noticed anything out of the ordinary with my skin. The only thing that might be related is that I've been feeling really hot and flushed, especially during these episodes, but I'm not sure if it's directly related to my skin or just a side effect of being so anxious about what's going on with my body.

turns.student: And your stomach — any queasiness or nausea during these moments?
turns.patient: To be honest, yeah, I get a bit queasy before and after an episode. Sometimes it feels like my stomach is churning or roiling, even when I'm not actually vomiting or anything. It's hard to describe, but it's this general feeling of discomfort in my gut that makes me feel really uncomfortable and anxious.

turns.student: What about other parts of your body? Any pain, tingling, or unusual sensations?
turns.patient: Honestly, besides the lower abdominal pain, I haven't noticed any other pains or unusual sensations in my body. The cramping is pretty much isolated to that area, and it's mostly left-sided. But to be honest, with all this diarrhea going on, I've been feeling really fatigued, like I'm running on fumes most of the time, especially after a big episode.

turns.student: Have you had any fevers, chills, or night sweats?
turns.patient: To be honest, yes. I've had some low-grade fever, usually when my symptoms are at their worst. It's not super high, but it's enough to make me feel really lousy - around 37.8-38.0°C a few times per week. And yeah, sometimes that comes with chills and night sweats, which is just great, because I'm already feeling pretty crummy most of the time.

turns.student: Now let's zoom out. Any swelling you've noticed anywhere on your body?
turns.patient: Honestly, no. I mean, I'm always exhausted and feel like I'm dragging myself around, but in terms of actual physical swelling or puffiness, I haven't noticed anything unusual.

turns.student: Have you noticed any changes in your weight or appetite?
turns.patient: To be honest, yeah, I have. Over the past three weeks, I've unintentionally lost about 4 kg (9 lbs), which is a pretty significant amount for me. It's been hard to keep up with my usual energy levels and it feels like I'm just wasting away, you know?

turns.student: Let me ask about your medical history. What conditions have you been treated for?
turns.patient: I've had an appendectomy when I was 16 years old, but that was a long time ago and there were no complications. Besides that, I don't think I've ever really had any serious health issues before this.

turns.student: Any medications you're taking? Are you taking them consistently?
turns.patient: I've been self-medicating with loperamide (Imodium) for diarrhea for the past 2 weeks, but to be honest, it hasn't really provided much relief. I've been trying to take it consistently, but sometimes I forget or skip a dose when my symptoms are particularly bad.

turns.student: Tell me about your lifestyle. How active are you?
turns.patient: To be honest, I'm not very active lately. My job as a software engineer at a startup has been really demanding, and I've been working long hours - sometimes over 60 hours a week. It's stressful, and I haven't had much time to exercise or take care of myself like I normally would. Mostly, I just end up coming home from work, eating whatever is easiest (usually takeout), and trying to rest before doing it all again the next day. It's not a great habit, but it's been hard to prioritize my health when I'm so busy with work.

turns.student: And your diet — what does a typical day of eating look like?
turns.patient: To be honest, it's usually whatever is easiest. With my long work hours, I've been relying on takeout and fast food a lot more than I normally would. Sometimes I'll grab something quick from a restaurant or get delivery from one of our startup's favorite spots. It's not exactly the healthiest option, but when you're working 12-hour days, it's hard to prioritize cooking meals at home.

turns.student: How about alcohol or smoking?
turns.patient: I do drink occasionally - usually 2-3 beers on weeknights, but I've actually increased that to 4-5 per night over the past month because of stress from my symptoms and work. But yeah, besides that, I'm a non-smoker.

turns.student: Now family history. Any medical problems run in your family?
turns.patient: My sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30, which is still really fresh for our whole family. It's been tough watching her go through that and it makes me a bit anxious about my own health right now.

turns.student: Specifically, has anyone in your family had serious or chronic conditions?
turns.patient: Yes, my older sister was diagnosed with Crohn's disease at age 30. It's been a tough experience watching her deal with it, and to be honest, it's made me a bit anxious about my own health right now, especially with these symptoms I'm experiencing.

turns.student: We've covered a lot. Is there anything we haven't discussed that you think is relevant?
turns.patient: You know, one thing that might be relevant but hasn't come up yet is my travel history. I was in Mexico for a work conference about three months ago, and while I had no GI issues during or after the trip, it's possible that something could have been triggered then. But honestly, I don't think that's directly related to what's going on with me now, and we've already covered so much ground.
